subject: The 1990 Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum heist — the largest unsolved art theft in history
central_question: How did two men in fake police uniforms walk out of a Boston museum with half a billion dollars of masterpieces — and why, decades later, has no one ever been caught and not a single painting recovered?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands exactly how the heist was pulled off in 81 minutes, what was taken and why it can never simply be sold, every serious theory about who did it, and why the empty frames still hang on the museum walls today.
surprise: Most people assume a theft this big must eventually be solved. In fact the thieves were never caught, the art was never found, the legal clock to prosecute the theft has run out, and the museum is required by its founder's will to leave the empty frames hanging exactly where the paintings were cut out.
stakes: The fate of thirteen irreplaceable works (including the only seascape Rembrandt ever painted and a Vermeer), a standing $10 million reward, and a mystery that has outlived its own suspects.
target_audience: English-speaking general audience that loves true-crime, heists, unsolved mysteries, and art/history
demand_evidence: Heist and unsolved-mystery content is one of the most reliable evergreen genres; this is the single most famous art theft in the world.
A genuine open mystery (never solved, art still missing, active reward) sustains rewatch and long-tail search interest.
competition: Plenty of coverage exists, but much of it either sensationalizes a single suspect or gets lost in mob-trivia; few neutrally walk the heist mechanics, the art, every theory, and the legal reason it can't be sold.
differentiation: Treats the Gardner heist as a clean, cinematic unsolved mystery: minute-by-minute reconstruction, the art explained, theories weighed without accusing any living person, and the haunting empty-frames ending — primary-source (FBI/museum) based, neutral, advertiser-safe, symbolic visuals with no real-person likeness. A pure heist anchor distinct from the channel's fraud and rights clusters.
research_feasibility: High: the FBI maintains a public Gardner case page; the museum publishes a theft page and the list of stolen works; decades of reputable reporting corroborate.
visual_feasibility: High: symbolic reconstruction of a night museum, fake-police ruse, cut canvases, empty gilded frames, a getaway, a map of theories — all achievable with no real-person likeness and without copying the real museum's identifiable interior.
risk_notes: Unsolved case. NEVER assert that any living person committed the theft. Use 'suspect', 'person of interest', 'investigated', 'never charged', and attribute every theory to its source. The FBI has said it believes the likely perpetrators are deceased — state that as the FBI's position, attributed.
Name persons of interest only as they appear in FBI statements / court records / strong reporting, with their exact status (deceased vs living; suspected, not convicted of the heist).

Stolen artworks are public domain (artists long deceased), but visuals are symbolic reconstruction, not authentic reproductions; do not depict the real museum's identifiable interior, logo, or architecture (use archetypal gallery imagery).
The museum is a victim; keep neutral and factual. The ~$500M figure is an estimate; the works are effectively unsellable.
No real-person likeness in any AI visual (invariant 11). Generated visuals never presented as authentic footage of the crime.
kill_conditions: Cannot source the heist mechanics, the list of stolen works, and the unsolved status to FBI/museum plus independent reporting.
Cannot frame the suspect theories without risking a defamation claim against a living person.
